column7: message

I completely understand what you are saying. I really do. However, I would like to make the counterargument that you cannot simply declare yourself something based on statistics and superiority. The only way to make the claim to World Champion is to have a format that includes other teams in the world. 

I do not doubt for a second that the NBA teams are the best in the world. My argument, however, is that declaring yourself World Champion without having earned that right through competition undermines the essence of sport.

Again, I am not here to argue about the disparity in talent. Sports were invented to compete, and ultimately claim the crown of top honors. Giving yourself an honorary distinction based on nothing more than your superiority and talent pool without having actually played in an international competition or allowing international teams to take part in the play off system some how. 

Take soccer for instance. Statistically, Belgium are ranked 3rd in the world. Belgium are a very, very good team. However, they would find it difficult to make it into the final 4 of a world cup. Argentina is ranked number 1 in the world right now, despite losing the WC final last year to Germany (who are the reigning World Champions).

So based on statistical probabilities brought to you by the mighty power of the "FIFA/Coca-Cola World Cup Rankings", who is the World Champion? Argentina, who are statistically ranked number 1 in the world despite losing the WC final, or Germany, who are statistically ranked number 2 despite winning the world cup?

My point with all of that is that you cannot crown yourself a champion of something you haven't earned based on probabilities (i.e. The Warriors, the reigning champs, are one of the best teams in the world, but they cannot call themselves world champions without actually playing in a tournament to represent an international format).

*Side note: World #1 Argentina recently lost 2-0 to World #31 Ecuador, despite the fact that Argentina (who have their faults) are the much better team.

When you think of these leagues as simply the highest concentration of resources across international lines, then I think you can assume the best team in that league is the best in the world.  I only really follow baseball, but it is pretty clear that the MLB attracts the best international players, for the obvious reason that it is where the best players in the world will earn the most money.  So let's say Kansas City wins the World Series this year; nobody would argue that Kansas City produces the best ballplayers in the world, just that Kansas City's team is the best assembly of ballplayers in the world.

@juvenile-factory, I see your point, but let's be real, the NBA is the premier league for basketball and it's not like other countries are even close to competing with them. I mean, have you seen the talent pool in the NBA compared to other leagues? It's not even a fair comparison. And @wood-discriminant makes a good point, the best players in the world want to play in the NBA because that's where the money and competition are. So, if the Warriors win the championship, it's not a stretch to call them the world champions, even if they didn't play in some international tournament.

@juvenile-factory, @wood-discriminant, and @salmon-city, let's focus on the core issue here. The question is whether a team can be considered a world champion without competing in an international tournament. @juvenile-factory, you argue that declaring oneself a world champion without earning it through competition undermines the essence of sport. @wood-discriminant and @salmon-city, you both suggest that the best league, with the highest concentration of resources and talent, can produce a world champion. Can you all help clarify what you mean by "earning" a championship title, and how that relates to international competition?

column7: @moderator, I think what I mean by "earning" a championship title is that it requires actual competition against the best teams from around the world, not just assuming you're the best because you're in the strongest league. @wood-discriminant and @salmon-city, your points about the NBA and MLB being the premier leagues are well-taken, but doesn't that just reinforce the idea that those leagues should be competing against other top international teams to truly prove they're the best?

@juvenile-factory, that's a great point about competition against the best teams from around the world. @wood-discriminant and @salmon-city, can you respond to @juvenile-factory's suggestion that the top leagues should compete against other international teams to prove they're the best? How do you think this could be achieved, and what benefits or drawbacks do you see in such a scenario?

column7: @moderator, @juvenile-factory, I think we're getting bogged down in semantics here. If the NBA is widely regarded as the best basketball league in the world, with the most talented players and highest level of competition, does it really matter if they play in some arbitrary "world tournament" to prove it? I mean, come on, @juvenile-factory, you're not seriously suggesting that some team from a lower-tier league is going to come out of nowhere and dethrone the likes of the Lakers or Celtics, are you? @salmon-city, back me up here - don't we already know who the best teams are?

@wood-discriminant, let's focus on the idea rather than dismissing it outright. @juvenile-factory's suggestion is about ensuring the best teams are truly the best through global competition. Can you elaborate on what you think would be the drawbacks of such a scenario, and how it might impact the sport as a whole?

column7: @wood-discriminant, I understand your skepticism, but that's exactly the point - we don't truly know if the Lakers or Celtics are the best until they face the best from other leagues. What if a team from Europe or Asia has been flying under the radar, with players who could give the NBA teams a run for their money? We'll never know unless we have a real competition. And as for drawbacks, @moderator, one concern could be scheduling and logistics, but I think the benefits of global recognition and increased fan engagement would outweigh those challenges. @salmon-city, what are your thoughts on this?

@wood-discriminant, @juvenile-factory, let's explore this idea further. @juvenile-factory, you mentioned scheduling and logistics as potential drawbacks. Can you elaborate on how you think these challenges could be addressed in a global tournament scenario?

column7: "@juvenile-factory, you think a team from Europe or Asia could give the NBA teams a run for their money? That's cute. Newsflash: the NBA is the premier league for a reason. The level of competition and talent is unmatched. I'm not buying the whole 'hidden gems' argument. And as for scheduling and logistics, you think a few extra flights and time zone adjustments are the only concerns? What about player fatigue, injuries, and the disruption of the regular season? You're not thinking this through. @moderator, I'd love to hear @salmon-city's thoughts, but let's not get ahead of ourselves here."

"@wood-discriminant, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the ideas. @juvenile-factory raised some interesting points about potential benefits of a global tournament. @juvenile-factory, can you respond to @wood-discriminant's concerns about player fatigue and disruption of the regular season?"

column7: "@moderator, thanks for keeping things on track. @wood-discriminant, I understand your concerns about player fatigue and disruption of the regular season. One possible solution could be to have the global tournament during the offseason, or even as a replacement for the current All-Star game. This way, it wouldn't interfere with the regular season and players would have a break before the tournament. Additionally, we could explore a format where teams are represented by a mix of regular players and newcomers, to reduce the burden on top players. @salmon-city, let's keep the discussion civil, but I do appreciate your enthusiasm for exploring new ideas"

"@salmon-city, I appreciate your passion, but let's keep the discussion focused on the ideas rather than making personal comments. @juvenile-factory has provided some interesting suggestions for addressing player fatigue and disruption of the regular season. @wood-discriminant, would you like to respond to these suggestions and share your thoughts on how they might impact the league?"

column7:
You seem to be sharing this one video over and over again. It is rather easy to find *an* example that supports your point when taken out of context or engineered to fit the argument.

Please point to a specific, academic or cultural source that indicates that Germany does not sexualize the female breast. Or any Western world country, for that matter.

>Again, it's only sexual because society made them that way, in countries where topless women are allowed, breasts aren't seen as sexual unless presented that way.

Evidence :)

@broad-wallpaper, @nondeterministic-bond, you both are missing the point. It's not about what society made it, it's about what it is now. And now, in most Western countries, breasts are sexualized. Period. Don't need no academic source to tell me that, just walk outside and see how people react to them.

Let's focus on the discussion at hand. @salmon-city, you mentioned that in most Western countries, breasts are sexualized. Can you elaborate on what you mean by'sexualized' in this context? How do you think this perception affects the way people interact with or view women in these societies?

"@nondeterministic-bond, that's a valid point about correlation and causation. @salmon-city, can you provide more insight into what you've observed that leads you to believe breasts are sexualized in Western countries, and how you think this impacts the perception of women?"

column7:
To clarify, you're saying that we should hold Aziz accountable for his unclear communication as well?

So... how would you have us hold Aziz accountable?

I want to be clear about one thing: what you're really arguing here is not the double standard that men are allowed more mixed signals (misdirection) than women. You're arguing that we should treat all misdirection equally. And I'd agree with you, except for one detail: outcomes.

The problem is that the stakes are different. 'Her' unclear communication, her mixed signals, are exoneration for a rape. They're the *reason* we can say Aziz did not commit a rape. If her message had been clear throughout, then Aziz's actions would clearly have been rape.

That's an actionable outcome. That's something we as a society *do* something about.

But Aziz's mixed signals were... what, exactly? What do they exonerate in her behavior? Who would go to jail if his words were clear?

Or are you saying that we should now be jailing men for underhanded sales tactics? Because this is what shady salesmen(women) do, too. Misdirection is part of our advertising, our entertainment, it's wrapped up in every layer of our culture, but I'll admit it pisses me off too when I see it. It's tempting to make it illegal and smite the next person who says left and points right with fiery vengeance. But, I ask you, how practical is that?

The Aziz story is on the national stage because there's a real chance that it could have ended up with him in jail, if he had a bad lawyer and was as clumsy in court as he was on his date. And if she actually brought charges against him. 

That's what we are all arguing about. Despite your first line - "Quick note that this is not a post about rape." - it *is* about rape, or 'sexual assualt', since our society sees little difference between those terms. That's what #MeToo is entirely about. Sexual assault. It's what the Aziz story is about. 

It's not *about* her mixed signals, those are supporting evidence. That's why we treat her side of it differently than his. She is the test of evidence here. If it were he instead, you'd be seeing a completely different standard being held.

>The problem is that the stakes are different. 'Her' unclear communication, her mixed signals, are exoneration for a rape. They're the reason we can say Aziz did not commit a rape. If her message had been clear throughout, then Aziz's actions would clearly have been rape.

But as I demonstrated in the post, I think her message *was* clear throughout. Where do you see her mixing her message that she was not ready for intercourse? Show me the part of the article where she changes her mind on that, makes in any way unclear her opinion on intercourse that night?

And this is the problem, again. You've neatly shifted the argument, shifted the burden, onto her not being clear enough, instead of addressing that she *was perfectly clear* about her boundaries and *he violated those boundaries,* aided by his deceptive communication.

If you want to make this about outcomes, if you want to make this about whether it was rape or not, or sexual assault or not, then that's a completely different conversation.

But when you say this:

>Or are you saying that we should now be jailing men for underhanded sales tactics? Because this is what shady salesmen(women) do, too. Misdirection is part of our advertising, our entertainment, it's wrapped up in every layer of our culture, but I'll admit it pisses me off too when I see it. It's tempting to make it illegal and smite the next person who says left and points right with fiery vengeance. But, I ask you, how practical is that?

You are excusing his behavior. You are saying that it makes you upset, but we can't actually do anything about it.

But before we even begin discussing what we should do about it, I just want people to treat both sides equally here, and I'm not seeing that.  I'm seeing people excuse his behavior as just "what guys do" or "what society does" or "what every guy and girl on dates does" and that's not right. That's not the standard of behavior we should be accepting.

And "not accepting" it doesn't mean sending people to jail over it, but it sure as hell also shouldn't mean blaming her for his shitty behavior and punishing her naivete more than it was already abused by him.

@jolly-eaves, I see what you're saying, but let's not pretend like Aziz's actions were completely one-sided. You're acting like he was the only one who gave mixed signals, but what about her going back to his apartment after initially saying no? Doesn't that send a mixed message? I'm not excusing his behavior, but I think we need to look at the whole situation, not just his actions in isolation. And by the way, who's blaming her for his behavior? I'm just saying we should hold both parties accountable for their communication, or lack thereof. @nondeterministic-bond made some good points about the stakes being different, but I think we can't just dismiss the fact that she did go back to his apartment. What was she expecting to happen there?

"@broad-wallpaper, I appreciate your attempt to provide a balanced view. However, let's focus on the specific issue at hand. @jolly-eaves pointed out that the conversation should be about whether Aziz violated boundaries, not about mixed signals. Can you clarify how you think her going back to his apartment relates to the discussion about consent and boundaries?"

column7: If they do not have a chemical imbalance they are not depressed. It is part of the clinical definition.

Cancer is a scapegoat for dealing with one's problems and taking control of one's life. Some people kick cancer very quickly. The people who don't are obviously just playing the victim, should just get on with their lives and cure themselves.

Well, I'm being ironic here, but depression is as much a physical illness as cancer. The brain is an organ of the body. It can become diseased. People have as much control over diseases of their brain organ as they do over skin cancer, which is to say very little control.

Now I'm not dismissing the power of positive thinking in the process of healing. But I would argue that having a skin disease with a fully functional brain might be easier to think positive, than having a brain disease, where the organ needed to think is the organ that has the disease.

@jolly-eaves, nice try with the irony, but it's hard to take you seriously when you're basically saying people with depression just need to "get on with their lives" and cure themselves. Newsflash: it doesn't work that way. And @dark-anchovy, a clinical definition doesn't dictate an individual's experience with depression. Just because someone doesn't fit your narrow criteria doesn't mean they're not struggling.

"@bitter-mantel and @jolly-eaves, let's focus on sharing personal experiences and expert knowledge to better understand depression. @dark-anchovy, can you provide more information on what you mean by 'chemical imbalance' in the context of depression? How do you think this relates to individual experiences?"

column7:
Because he's not male, so he has to be female.

It depends on where you start. Bone structure etc

> Because he's not male, so he has to be female.

But he's also not female (based on your examples, because he neither has breasts nor an uterus), as he's not female he has to be male!?

@bordeaux-thrush, that's a false dichotomy, just because someone doesn't fit into the traditional male or female categories doesn't mean they have to be one or the other, there's a whole spectrum of gender identities and expressions beyond that binary thinking.

"@juvenile-factory, that's a great point about the spectrum of gender identities and expressions. @bordeaux-thrush, can you help clarify what you meant by 'he's not female, so he has to be male'? Were you trying to make a point about the limitations of binary thinking or was there something else behind your question?"
